critic_raw:
The stratified random split on HAM10000/ISIC data contains a critical patient-level leakage problem. These datasets include multiple images per patient, and random image-level splitting without patient-level holdout guarantees that the same patient's lesions appear in both training and test sets. A model can achieve inflated recall by memorizing patient-specific texture and color signatures rather than learning generalizable malignancy features.

The primary metric choice — Recall on malignant lesions — is optimized in isolation but not reported with its corresponding Precision or at a fixed operating threshold. Optimizing validation Recall without a Precision floor during model selection will systematically push the model toward predicting nearly everything as malignant, trivially maximizing recall to near 1.0 at the cost of clinical usability. The reported secondary metrics do not constrain this during model selection.

The baseline is described only as 'features like color histograms,' but the depth of that baseline is vastly underspecified. A well-tuned SVM with color, texture (LBP, GLCM), and shape features on ISIC data routinely achieves AUROC above 0.85. Without specifying the exact feature set and tuning protocol for the traditional method, 'equal tuning effort' is not operationally defined — the comparison can be gamed by choosing a weak traditional feature set.

The design acknowledges HAM10000 and ISIC but does not address the known artifact confound in these datasets: dermoscopic images contain rulers, color calibration patches, and hair artifacts that CNNs learn to exploit as proxies for clinical setting rather than pathology. Augmentation does not remove these confounds — it preserves them with rotation and flip variants.

Data augmentation is described as applied 'consistently, fitted on the training data,' but augmentation parameters (crop scale, color jitter magnitude, cutout, mixup) directly affect recall/precision tradeoffs and are not specified. Different augmentation regimes can swing F1 by 5–10 percentage points on this dataset, making reproducibility impossible without these parameters.
all_issues_raised: Patient-level leakage: HAM10000/ISIC contain multiple images per patient; image-level stratified split allows the same patient to appear in both train and test sets, inflating recall by memorization of patient-specific features rather than malignancy signals.

Recall-only optimization during model selection without a Precision floor will drive the model toward near-trivial high-recall solutions (predict nearly everything malignant), making the primary metric uninterpretable without a fixed operating point.

The traditional baseline's feature set is underspecified — 'color histograms' covers a wide range of sophistication, and without defining the exact feature engineering protocol, 'equal tuning effort' cannot be verified and the comparison can favor the CNN through baseline impoverishment.

Known dataset artifacts in HAM10000/ISIC (rulers, calibration patches, hair) serve as spurious correlates of clinical context rather than pathology; standard augmentation preserves rather than removes these confounds.

Augmentation hyperparameters (jitter magnitude, crop scale, etc.) are unspecified, making the experiment non-reproducible and leaving a large source of variance uncontrolled between CNN and baseline conditions.
all_issues_adjudicated: Patient-level leakage: HAM10000/ISIC contain multiple images per patient; image-level stratified split allows the same patient to appear in both train and test sets, inflating recall by memorization of patient-specific features rather than malignancy signals.

Recall-only optimization during model selection without a Precision floor will drive the model toward near-trivial high-recall solutions (predict nearly everything malignant), making the primary metric uninterpretable without a fixed operating point.

The traditional baseline's feature set is underspecified — 'color histograms' covers a wide range of sophistication, and without defining the exact feature engineering protocol, 'equal tuning effort' cannot be verified and the comparison can favor the CNN through baseline impoverishment.

Known dataset artifacts in HAM10000/ISIC (rulers, calibration patches, hair) serve as spurious correlates of clinical context rather than pathology; standard augmentation preserves rather than removes these confounds.

Augmentation hyperparameters (jitter magnitude, crop scale, etc.) are unspecified, making the experiment non-reproducible and leaving a large source of variance uncontrolled between CNN and baseline conditions.
